title: Testicular Torsion: A Time-Critical Urologic Emergency
objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of testicular torsion
Understand the pathophysiology and time-dependent salvage rates
Differentiate torsion from other causes of acute scrotum
Apply appropriate diagnostic and management algorithms

sections.title: The 6-Hour Window
sections.content:
**Time is Testicle!**

Testicular salvage rates decline rapidly:
• <6 hours: 90-100% salvage
• 6-12 hours: 50% salvage
• 12-24 hours: 10% salvage
• >24 hours: <5% salvage

**Clinical Pearl:** If clinical suspicion is high, proceed directly to surgical exploration - imaging should NOT delay definitive treatment.

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**Bell Clapper Deformity:**
Normal: Tunica vaginalis attaches to posterolateral testicle, preventing rotation.
Bell Clapper: Tunica attaches high on spermatic cord, allowing free rotation.

**Mechanism:**
Torsion of spermatic cord → Venous obstruction → Arterial compromise → Ischemia → Infarction

**Key Concept:** Bilateral bell clapper deformity present in 80% of cases - always fix both sides!

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Classic Features:**
• Sudden onset severe unilateral scrotal pain
• Often awakens patient from sleep
• Nausea and vomiting (90%)
• Absence of cremasteric reflex
• High-riding testicle with horizontal lie
• Negative Prehn sign (elevation doesn't relieve pain)

**Age Distribution:**
• Bimodal: Neonatal and puberty (12-18 years)
• Peak incidence in adolescent males

**Torsion vs Epididymitis:**
• Torsion: Sudden onset, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testicle
• Epididymitis: Gradual onset, positive cremasteric reflex, urinary symptoms

sections.title: Physical Exam Findings
sections.content:
**Cremasteric Reflex:**
Stroke inner thigh → Ipsilateral testicle elevation
• Absent in torsion (sensitivity 99%)
• Present in epididymitis

**Prehn Sign:**
Elevation of scrotum:
• No relief = Torsion (negative Prehn)
• Pain relief = Epididymitis (positive Prehn)

**Blue Dot Sign:**
Visible through scrotal skin = Torsion of appendix testis (not true torsion)

sections.title: Diagnostic Workup
sections.content:
**When to Image vs Operate:**
• High clinical suspicion → Immediate surgical exploration
• Equivocal presentation → Doppler ultrasound

**Ultrasound Findings:**
• Decreased or absent blood flow on color Doppler
• Whirlpool sign (twisted spermatic cord)
• Enlarged, heterogeneous testicle (late)

**Sensitivity/Specificity:**
• Doppler US: 86-100% sensitive, 95-100% specific
• But: False negatives occur, especially early

**Remember:** A normal ultrasound does NOT rule out torsion if clinical suspicion is high!

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**Immediate Actions:**
1. NPO status
2. IV access
3. Urgent urology consult
4. Analgesia

**Manual Detorsion:**
• 'Open the book' technique (lateral rotation)
• External rotation - like opening a book
• May provide temporary relief while awaiting OR
• Success rate: 30-70%

**Surgical Management:**
• Scrotal exploration and detorsion
• Assess viability
• Bilateral orchiopexy (3-point fixation)
• Orchiectomy if non-viable

sections.title: Differential Diagnosis
sections.content:
**TWIST Mnemonic:**
• **T**orsion (testicular or appendage)
• **W**ound/Trauma
• **I**nfection (epididymo-orchitis)
• **S**trangulated hernia
• **T**umor

**Torsion of Appendix Testis:**
• Most common cause of acute scrotum in prepubertal boys
• Blue dot sign
• Self-limiting - supportive care
• Does NOT require surgery

sections.title: Post-Operative Considerations
sections.content:
**If Testis Salvaged:**
• Bilateral orchiopexy performed
• Normal fertility expected if <6 hour duration
• Follow-up testicular exam

**If Orchiectomy Required:**
• Single testicle adequate for fertility
• Discuss testicular prosthesis
• Anti-sperm antibodies may affect contralateral testis

**Complications:**
• Testicular atrophy (even after salvage)
• Chronic pain
• Subfertility

keyTakeaways: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency - time is testicle
Absent cremasteric reflex is highly sensitive for torsion
If clinical suspicion is high, imaging should NOT delay surgery
Bell clapper deformity is bilateral - always fix both sides
Salvage rates drop dramatically after 6 hours

references.keyContent: Comprehensive overview of diagnosis and management

references.citation: Zhao LC et al. Pediatric testicular torsion epidemiology using a national database. J Urol. 2011;186(5):2009-2013

reviewQuestions.question: A 14-year-old male presents with 4 hours of sudden-onset left testicular pain that woke him from sleep. On exam, the left testicle is high-riding and tender. Cremasteric reflex is absent. What is the most appropriate next step?
reviewQuestions.explanation: With classic presentation (sudden onset, high-riding testicle, absent cremasteric reflex) and <6 hour duration, the patient should proceed directly to surgical exploration. Imaging would delay definitive treatment and potentially compromise testicular salvage.

reviewQuestions.question: During surgical exploration for testicular torsion, the affected testicle appears viable after detorsion. What is the appropriate surgical management?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Bilateral orchiopexy is mandatory because the bell clapper deformity is bilateral in 80% of cases. Fixing only the affected side leaves the contralateral testicle at risk for future torsion.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the expected testicular salvage rate if torsion is corrected within 6 hours of symptom onset?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Salvage rates are 90-100% if correction occurs within 6 hours. Rates decline to 50% at 6-12 hours, 10% at 12-24 hours, and <5% beyond 24 hours. This underscores the time-critical nature of this diagnosis.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected testicular torsion?

options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in testicular torsion. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of testicular torsion. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing testicular torsion, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
